title: Plantain leaf cough syrup
subtitle: A soothing syrup for irritable dry cough
excerpt: A strong infusion of dried plantain leaf combined with honey to make a soothing herb cough syrup. Traditionally prepared for dry, irritating coughs and sore, inflamed throats.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, plantain entry: the cough and throat preparation, the gel-forming soothing action. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Plantago major, the soothing herb use for mucus and congestion and irritated mucous membranes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in a sealed jar in the fridge. Use within 2 weeks; the honey preserves the infusion but does not keep it indefinitely.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that soothes and protects inflamed or irritated mucous membranes. Plantain's mucilage coats the throat and upper airways.

content.text: Plantain leaf syrup is a traditional remedy for the dry, scratchy cough that comes with a cold or follows a bout of laryngitis. The

soothing gel in the plantain leaf soothes the inflamed airways; the honey acts as a preservative and adds its own mild throat-soothing properties. The preparation is gentle enough for the whole family (except infants under one year, for whom honey is not safe). Making a small batch of 200 ml takes about 35 minutes and keeps 2 weeks in the fridge.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Honey and infants. Do not give honey-containing preparations to children under one year because of the risk of infant botulism.

Plantain is one of the safest herbs in domestic use and has no significant drug interactions at this dose.

Coughs that need medical attention. A cough with blood-streaked mucus, a cough with significant breathlessness or chest pain, a cough that persists for more than 3 weeks without improvement, or a cough with significant fever should be assessed by a doctor before any herbal treatment.

items.prepNote: dried broadleaf or ribwort plantain; both work
raw honey is traditional but any runny honey works

content.text: Place the dried plantain in a small saucepan. Pour over the hot water. Cover and steep for 10-12 minutes. The infusion should be pale brown and smell gently herbal. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve lined with muslin if available, pressing the spent herb firmly to extract all the liquid. You should have about 200-220 ml of strong infusion.

Return the strained infusion to the saucepan and warm gently over low heat to about 40-50°C (the infusion should be warm to the touch but not hot). Add the honey and stir until fully dissolved. Do not boil: honey above 60°C loses its beneficial enzymes and the flavour changes. Pour into a sterilised 200 ml glass jar with a lid. Allow to cool before sealing.

Adults and children over one year: 1-2 teaspoons (5-10 ml) up to four times a day, as needed for the cough. For children aged 1-7, 1 teaspoon maximum, less frequently. Give at the onset of a coughing fit or before bed if the cough disturbs sleep. Do not exceed 4 doses in 24 hours. Use for the duration of the acute episode (typically 5-7 days). If the cough persists beyond 2-3 weeks, seek medical advice.

Store in a sealed glass jar in the fridge. Use within 2 weeks. If the syrup smells fermented, looks cloudy beyond natural haze, or develops any surface mould, discard and make a fresh batch. The honey preserves the infusion but does not make it shelf-stable at room temperature.
When not to use this remedy
Infants under one year (honey risk). Coughs with blood, significant breathlessness, stridor, or high fever need immediate medical assessment.
